text: You missed the deadline. This is on you.
Although the deadline slipped, I know you were working with a broken forecast.

Thanks for joining. I want to be candid, but I also want to lead with empathy. Although the deadline slipped, I know you were working with a broken forecast.
Honestly, it feels like we are getting the blame for something that was not our fault.
I hear you, and I am not here to blame anyone. While I understand the pressure, the slip did create real friction with the client.
Fair. I got a bit defensive -- I did not want it to look like we overstepped. So what would help?
Despite the friction, I think we can find common ground. The forecast failed, whereas your team responded well. The accountability sits with the process, not the people.
That I can work with. Even though it means more reporting, a shared tracking standard would de-escalate this for good.
Although the deadline slipped, I know the forecast was broken.
While I understand the pressure, the slip did hurt the client.
The accountability sits with the process, not the people.
Lead with empathy, and the hard feedback lands.

Morning, everyone. Before the review meeting, a quick word on how we handle the disagreement with the logistics team. There is clearly some friction, and a couple of people have raised a grievance about how the last delay was managed. Our job today is to de-escalate, not to win. So please, no matter how strong your view is, lead with empathy. Even though the team missed the deadline, they were working with a broken forecast, so we acknowledge that first. Acknowledge the other side, and then make your point: yes, the slip hurt us, but the root cause sits with the process, not the people. Be candid about what went wrong, while staying respectful about who was involved. Where someone gets defensive, do not push harder; ask a question instead. We want accountability, not blame. And although we have to be assertive about the new standard, we do it without leaving anyone holding a grudge. A team that feels heard is a team that fixes the problem. Let us turn this standoff into a shared plan.

Hi Marcus, this is Fabiana with a quick update after the review meeting. It went better than I expected. There was a lot of friction at the start, and the logistics lead got quite defensive when I raised the missed deadline. So I changed my approach. Although I had to be candid about the impact, I led with empathy and acknowledged that they were working with a broken forecast. That helped me de-escalate. Whereas last time the conversation ended in a standoff, this time we found common ground. I was honest about the slip, but I kept the focus on the process rather than the people, so nobody walked away with a grudge. We agreed on shared accountability for the new tracking standard. There is still a bit of resentment under the surface, so I would suggest one short follow-up, just to make sure the goodwill holds. I will send my notes tonight. Thanks, and talk soon.

Marcus briefs the team and asks them to de-escalate the friction rather than win. Fabiana opens the meeting by leading with empathy and acknowledging the broken forecast. The logistics lead gets defensive when the missed deadline is raised. Fabiana stays candid about the impact while keeping the focus on the process, not the people. Both sides reach common ground and agree on shared accountability.
